elements.sent-2411-001: This paper presents comparison results and establishes risk bounds for credit portfolios within classes of Bernoulli mixture models, assuming conditionally independent defaults that are stochastically increasing with a common risk factor.
elements.sent-2411-002: We provide simple and interpretable conditions for conditional default probabilities that imply a comparison of credit portfolio losses in convex order.
elements.sent-2411-003: In the case of threshold models, the ranking of portfolio losses is based on a pointwise comparison of the underlying copulas.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our setting includes as special case the well-known Gaussian copula model but allows for general tail dependencies, which are crucial for modeling credit portfolio risks.
elements.sent-2411-005: Moreover, our results extend the classical parameterized models, such as the industry models CreditMetrics and KMV Portfolio Manager, to a robust setting where individual parameters or the copula modeling the dependence structure can be ambiguous.
elements.sent-2411-006: A simulation study and a real data example under model uncertainty offer evidence supporting the effectiveness of our approach.
elements.sent-2411-007: A fundamental problem in modeling credit portfolio risk is that defaults are rare events, resulting in very limited available data.
elements.sent-2411-008: While the default probabilities of individual entities can generally be estimated with reasonable accuracy, e.g. using historical data or financial and macroeconomic indicators, and can therefore be treated as known, modeling the typically positive dependencies between default events poses a greater challenge.
elements.sent-2411-009: These dependencies, driven by common exposure to economic cycles, market shocks, and industry-specific risk factors, are complex to capture accurately due to the curse of dimensionality.
elements.sent-2411-010: For this reason, factor models for credit portfolio risk have been introduced, which are economically interpretable, easier to estimate, have well understood properties and are reliable under different scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-011: Well-known examples of industry credit risk models are CreditMetrics (cf.
elements.sent-2411-012: The first two models belong to the class of Bernoulli mixture models where default is described by the event when the firm's latent asset return falls below a given threshold reflecting its liabilities.
elements.sent-2411-013: In contrast, the CreditRisk$^+$ model is an actuarial approach that approximates the Bernoulli default indicator with a Poisson random variable, resulting in a Poisson mixture model as an approximation of the Bernoulli mixture model.
elements.sent-2411-014: However, due to the scarcity of data, estimating the dependencies between defaults of different entities remains a challenging task, even in factor models.
elements.sent-2411-015: This leads to a certain amount of ambiguity in some parameter estimates such as the asset correlation coefficient.
elements.sent-2411-016: Additionally, asset returns often exhibit tail dependencies, which are typically underestimated by Gaussian factor models.
elements.sent-2411-017: Consequently, tail risks are likely to be underrepresented in portfolio losses.
elements.sent-2411-018: These challenges highlight the need to study robustness in credit risk models, accounting for both tail dependencies and model uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-019: In this paper, we establish robustness results for Bernoulli mixture models, assuming that the conditional default probabilities of individual borrowers increase with a common risk factor, which may be either a latent random variable or an observable random variable, such as a market index.
elements.sent-2411-020: Our main contribution, Theorem \ref{propcomBMMs , is a comparison result for credit portfolio losses where we provide straightforward and interpretable conditions on the conditional default probability functions that lead to a convex ordering of the losses.}
elements.sent-2411-021: More specifically, the loss variable of portfolio $A$ is smaller in convex order than that of portfolio $B$ if the default events in portfolio $B$ exhibit a stronger positive dependence on the (potentially portfolio-specific) common risk factor than those in portfolio $A$.
elements.sent-2411-022: The proof relies on a general construction of stochastically increasing Bernoulli mixture models via copula-based threshold models.
elements.sent-2411-023: As an additional contribution of our paper, we provide a comparison of credit portfolio losses in convex order through a pointwise comparison of the copulas underlying the threshold models (Proposition \ref{lemconord ).}
elements.sent-2411-024: We then address model uncertainty by establishing lower and upper bounds for credit portfolio losses in convex order (Theorem \ref{mainthe ) and for credit portfolio risks in terms of law-invariant, convex risk measures (Corollary \ref{cormaithe ).}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Our robustness results enable a simple comparison of different models, allowing risk measures to be ranked across various model specifications.
elements.sent-2411-026: While most of the paper assumes that default probabilities increase with the common risk factor, Theorem \ref{thebmmsiBMM presents a robustness result that holds even without this monotonicity assumption.}
elements.sent-2411-027: We illustrate our results in two contexts: first, when individual parameters are ambiguous, and second, when there is uncertainty about the copula modeling the dependence between default events.
elements.sent-2411-028: In both cases, we calculate bounds for the Average Value-at-Risk (AVaR), a benchmark risk measure in the Basel III and Solvency II frameworks, using both simulated and real portfolios.

elements.sent-2411-206: In the following section, we apply our robustness results to data and study improved bounds for the AVaR for families of siBMMs under parameter uncertainty and for classes of siBMMs under model uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-207: We illustrate our findings on the robustness of credit portfolio models with a simulation study and a real data example.
elements.sent-2411-208: First, we perform a simulation study considering four different classes of threshold models.
elements.sent-2411-209: The first one, the Gaussian copula model, is a standard copula model but has the drawback that it does not admit tail dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-210: Threshold models, as discussed in Section \ref{secmain , define the default indicator by the event where the value of a financial asset drops below a specified threshold, typically corresponding to the company's liability value.}
elements.sent-2411-211: Given the strong tail dependencies commonly observed in financial assets, copula models that account for such dependencies are generally more appropriate than the Gaussian copula model.
elements.sent-2411-212: For this reason, we specifically examine the Clayton copula family, which belongs to the class of Archimedean copulas, along with its survival copulas.
elements.sent-2411-213: As a fourth class, we examine a hybrid approach that combines Gaussian and Clayton copula models.
elements.sent-2411-214: We then compare the bounds derived under model uncertainty for this union with the individual bounds obtained under parameter uncertainty for each copula model.

elements.sent-2411-240: In the settings of the default models analysed in Example \ref{exthreshmod , our simulations demonstrate that accurately capturing tail dependencies is more critical than accounting for parameter uncertainty within the individual models.}
elements.sent-2411-241: This is evidenced by the non-overlapping uncertainty intervals observed in the Gaussian, Clayton, and survival Clayton copula settings.
elements.sent-2411-242: The lower bound of the uncertainty interval in the survival Clayton copula setting is only marginally higher than the risk \(\avar_\alpha^\perp := \avar_\alpha(L^\perp)\) of the credit portfolio with independent default events.
elements.sent-2411-243: This can be attributed to the fact that the lower tail dependencies in the survival Clayton copula are nearly independent (as evident from the lower left corner of the sample generated from the survival Clayton copula in Figure \ref{fig:sampleCopulaModels ).}
elements.sent-2411-244: In contrast, the Clayton copula setting captures lower tails with a notably strong positive dependence, effectively accounting for the pronounced co-movements in extreme events (see the lower left corner of the sample from the Clayton copula in Figure \ref{fig:sampleCopulaModels ).}
elements.sent-2411-245: As a consequence, the upper bound for the Clayton copula is about two third of the worst-case comonotonic bound \(\avar_\alpha^c := \avar_\alpha(L^c)\,.\) The tail dependencies in the Gaussian setting lie somewhere in between, suggesting that portfolio risks modeled using the Gaussian model might initially appear to provide a realistic approach.
elements.sent-2411-246: However, as previously noted, Gaussian copulas typically underestimate tail dependencies in financial applications.
elements.sent-2411-247: Consequently, the Clayton copula setting may offer a more accurate reflection of the associated risks.
elements.sent-2411-248: To find a balance between the Gaussian and the Clayton copula settings, we introduce a class of distributions whose tail dependencies lie between those of the specified Gaussian and Clayton copula.
elements.sent-2411-249: More precisely, we consider a class siBMMs, where the default integral functions are generated by a combination of Gaussian and Clayton copula threshold models discussed before.

elements.sent-2411-279: In this paper, we established a convex ordering result for credit portfolio losses in stochastically increasing Bernoulli mixture models based on transparent and interpretable assumptions on the conditional default probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-280: For threshold models, we provided a related ordering result based on a pointwise comparison of bivariate copulas.
elements.sent-2411-281: As a consequence, we obtained several robustness results for credit portfolio losses.
elements.sent-2411-282: Without assuming monotonicity of the conditional default probabilities in the common factor, we derived an upper bound on portfolio losses that improves the standard comonotonic bound.
elements.sent-2411-283: A detailed simulation study and a real-data example illustrate the relevance and effectiveness of our approach.
elements.sent-2411-284: Our methods are especially applicable to standard industry models of credit portfolio risk, such as CreditMetrics or KMV Portfolio Manager, and allow for the incorporation of tail dependencies and model uncertainty into these models.
elements.sent-2411-285: In this way, our paper provides tractable and practically relevant methods for the assessment of credit portfolio risk under mild structural assumptions.

elements.sent-2411-308: We obtain from Lemma \ref{correpBMM that \(X_n,Y\sim U(0,1)\) and \(C_{X_n,Y}\) is SI for all \(n\,.\) Further, \(X_1,\ldots,X_N\) are conditionally independent given \(Y\) and we have \(D_n = \1_{\{X_n \leq c_n\}}\) for all \(n\,.\) Similarly, consider random variables \((X_n')_n\) and \(Y'\) associated with the default model \((D_1',\ldots,D_N',Z')\,.\) Then, we obtain for all \((u,v)\in [0,1]^2\) and for all \(n\) that \begin{equation}C_{X_n,Y}(u,v) = \int_0^v P(X_n\leq u \mid Y = y) \de y = 1 - \int_v^1 P(X_n\leq u \mid Y = y) \de y =  1 - \int_v^1 \frac{u}{c_n} P(D_n = 1 \mid Z = F_Z^{-1}(1-y)) \de y\,, if  u \leq c_n\,, 1 - \int_v^1 \left[\frac{u-c_n}{1-c_n} + \left(\frac{u}{c_n} - \frac{u-c_n}{1-c_n}\right) P(D_n=1\mid Z = F_Z^{-1}(1-y))\right] \de y\,, if  u > c_n\,,  =  1 - \frac{u}{c_n} \int_0^{1-v} P(D_n = 1 \mid Z = F_Z^{-1}(y')) \de y'\,, if  u \leq c_n\,, 1-\frac{(1-v)(u-c_n)}{1-c_n} -\left(\frac{u}{c_n}-\frac{u-c_n}{1-c_n}\right) \int_0^{1-v} P(D_n = 1 \mid Z = F_Z^{-1}(y')) \de y' \,, if  u > c_n\,,  =  1 - \frac{u}{c_n} G_{n,Z}(1-v)\,, if  u \leq c_n\,, 1-\frac{(1-v)(u-c_n)}{1-c_n} -\left(\frac{u}{c_n}-\frac{u-c_n}{1-c_n}\right) G_{D_n,Z}(1-v) \,, if  u > c_n\,,  \leq  1 - \frac{u}{c_n} G_{n,Z'}(1-v)\,, if  u \leq c_n\,, 1-\frac{(1-v)(u-c_n)}{1-c_n} -\left(\frac{u}{c_n}-\frac{u-c_n}{1-c_n}\right) G_{D_n,Z'}(1-v) \,, if  u > c_n\,,  = \cdots = C_{X_n',Y'}(u,v)\,,\end{equation} where the third equality follows from \eqref{correpBMM1 .}}
